references.title: First‐Year Engineering Students’ Strategies for Taking Exams
references.journal: International Journal of Artificial Intelligence in Education

extracted.Courses: First Year Courses with exam at a Mechanical Engineering Program
extracted.Features: [Sequence of Exams Taken and corresponding Grade foir Each Week]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Out of the initial 361 students, authors filtered out 50. 

Exam attempt logs consider,  for each exam, one of 4 possible situations:

0 - not taking, 
1. Failed first attempt, 
2. Failed Second Attempt, 
3. Passed

Whithin each week, the student is represented by one of 64 possible states. The input to the Markov Chain is of length 6. Similarity between the groups is done via shortest-path kernel method.
extracted.Cluster Description:
Successful Students: Passed all 3 exams

Passing Students: 1 or 2 exams

Failing Students: Failed on all 3 exams
extracted.Implications:
Successful students start strongly with the attempts at the start of the exam season.

Unsuccessful students start slow and tend to accelerate in their attempts with the passage of time. 

When it comes to exam-taking behavior - Pace is the largest distinctor between successful and unsuccessful students.
extracted.Comments:
Authors klook at exam-taking behvaior of first-year students on the first semester exam season. 

To do that, they take Exam Attempt Logs and, for each week of the exam-taking season, the state of attempts is evaluated. 

The transitions of behavior across the weeks show that succesful student work at a much higher pace than their peers.

AS a matter of fact, the speed at which learners attempt and take exams is the most relevaant predictor.

The findings were used in a subsequent exam season to provide soime intervention.
extracted.Limitations:
Not generalizable to a siituation where an exam has to be taken on a specific date-time.

Complexity analysis could be more developed.

Room for addition of data from other sources.

annotations.text: Dropout is a big concern, especially among first-year students who may not be ready for the transition between their previous educational settings to the new ones. The work focuses on exploring first-year students’ exam-taking patterns using layered Markov-Chain probabilistic graphs.
